data.title: D.C. Is Having A Wild Year, But NVR Is Still A Buy
data.description:
In February 2025, I wrote an article about a new stock I had recently discovered. The stock had a unique, low-debt business model, consistent net income growth, and a track record of excellent capital allocation. Based on the comments I received, many other investors also saw the potential for robust growth ahead for NVR, Inc. (NYSE: NYSE:NVR).

However, I also received a large number of comments from readers asking one particular question: What about the Washington D.C. market?

NVR is based in Reston, VA, approximately 30 minutes east of Washington D.C., and that metro accounts for a decent share of revenue for the company. And so far in 2025, Washington, D.C. has had a bizarre year.

In January 2025, President Donald Trump and the White House released an Executive Order establishing DOGE, the Department of Government Efficiency, and announced that there would be a restructuring of the federal workforce. The task force was led by Elon Musk, with a particular goal of slashing federal spending by directing agencies to work with DOGE to downsize their workforce, overhaul hiring, and centralize spending oversight.

Later that same month, the White House unleashed ICE agents to crack down on undocumented immigrants, making thousands of arrests, with a large number coming from the D.C. area. Officials aim to arrest undocumented immigrants who’ve committed crimes, but critics argue that innocent migrants are also at risk of being detained.

Then, in February, President Trump called for a federal takeover of D.C. governance, stating the local authorities were "not doing the job" regarding the crime in Washington D.C. Trump went on to tell reporters on Air Force One, “I think we should take over Washington, D.C. — make it safe. I think that we should govern the District of Columbia."

And then on August 11, President Trump invoked Section 740 of the Home Rule Act, declaring a public safety emergency in Washington, D.C. He simultaneously federalized control of the Metropolitan Police Department and ordered the deployment of an additional 800 National Guard troops.

It’s no wonder that so many investors are wondering what in the world is going on in Washington, D.C., and more importantly, what does all this mean for NVR.

So, let’s go through them.

DOGE In D.C.

In my opinion, there is no question that the DOGE cuts have affected the Washington D.C. community. The federal government makes up an inordinate amount of the workforce in Washington, D.C., accounting for approximately 24.6% of jobs. No other market in the U.S. comes close to that number. For context, number two is Maryland at 5.4%, and Virginia is number three with 4.4%.

In July, the Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond released a report on the regional changes of the federal workforce in the D.C. region. According to their evaluation, Washington D.C. had 380,000 federal employees in December 2024, and by May 2025, that number had shrunk to 364,200.

Naturally, unemployment claims began to rise during that time, but what was more alarming to me was how high continued claims seemed to persist in Washington, D.C.

Based on the continued claims, it would appear that the laid-off employees in D.C. are taking longer to find new employment. So, in my view, it is clear that the DOGE cuts have certainly had an impact on the employment in Washington, D.C. for the federal workforce. However, to understand the impact this may have on NVR and Washington D.C. residents' ability and appetite for buying new homes, we have to look at the overall employment picture.

In June 2025, Washington, D.C. hit an unemployment rate of 6.2%, slightly elevated from 5.6% in January 2025.

While it does appear that the unemployment rate has been rising over the last few years, it does not appear to me that the federal cuts have dramatically impacted the overall unemployment rate in the region.

However, unemployment is just one piece of a much larger picture for the housing market in Washington, D.C. Another component is to understand what the population in D.C. is doing.

Immigration & Migration

According to the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement website, ICE enforcement has had a significant impact on arrests and removals of undocumented citizens in the United States. However, the impact on the Washington D.C. market, in my view, has been relatively small when looking at the entire population of the region. So far in 2025, ICE has made 615 arrests and 466 removals in Washington, D.C.

In 2024, Census Data showed the Washington D.C. population at 702,250 residents, which would represent an increase of 15,000 residents from 2023. The growth came from a combination of natural increases as well as migration. International migration rose by 21% when compared to the previous year, while outmigration decreased, with only 337 residents leaving D.C. in 2024.

Migration patterns are slow-moving targets, and tend to take several months or even years to materialize, so if there is a potential mass exodus coming in the D.C. area, it would theoretically take a few quarters to show up in the data. However, in my view, the migration patterns in Washington, D.C., seem to bode well for homebuilders like NVR for now. It would appear that even with the job losses from the DOGE cuts and the effects of ICE arrests and removals, the overall population for D.C. is still growing.

But what about the crime?

The Crime In D.C.

On August 11, 2025, President Trump announced he would be placing Washington, D.C. under federal control, deploying National Guard Troops to take over the Metropolitan Police Department. Trump also displayed several charts showing the homicide rate in D.C. compared to select cities around the world.

In his speech, Trump said: "The murder rate in Washington today is higher than that of Bogotá, Colombia; Mexico City, or some of the places that you hear about as being the worst places on Earth. It’s much higher."

So, what is the crime rate in Washington, D.C.?

According to Metropolitan Police data, the crime rate in D.C. is improving. For 2025, year-to-date, all crime is 16,479 cases against 17,832 this same time last year, a reduction of 8%. Violent crime is down even more, dropping from 2,246 cases by this time last year compared to 1,640 cases this year, a reduction of 27%.

The full-year 2024 numbers show similar improvements. Violent crime and total crimes are down 35% and 15%, respectively. In Trump’s speeches, he has been specifically calling out the homicide rate, but there are still signs that show homicides seem to be improving, too. RIT released a 15-page review of homicides in 24 U.S. Cities, which showed Washington, D.C. with 27.3 homicides in 2024 compared to 39.4 per 100,000 people in 2023.

What’s clear to me is that crime is always something that can be improved in any large city, but I do not see any red flags that might impact NVR’s business from the potential crime happening in D.C. Although, the recent turmoil created by the White House is certainly making things in D.C. a bit bizarre, as an investor I do not think this will have a material impact on NVR’s ability to sell homes.

As a backdrop to all of this weirdness in D.C., let’s also not forget how much business NVR actually does in D.C.

How Much Does D.C. Even Matter?

NVR’s headquarters are based in Reston, VA, about 30 minutes east of Washington D.C., and sells homes in the following four segments.

  • Mid Atlantic: Maryland, Virginia, West Virginia, Delaware, and Washington, D.C.
  • North East: New Jersey and Eastern Pennsylvania
  • Mid East: New York, Ohio, Western Pennsylvania, Indiana, and Illinois
  • South East: North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia, Florida, and Tennessee

NVR does not provide numbers for D.C. specifically, but in 2024, these regions made up the following as a percentage of revenue:

  • Mid Atlantic: 45.0%
  • North East: 10.1%
  • Mid East: 18.5%
  • South East: 26.4%

The Mid-Atlantic region would be the closest number available to indicate if a potential slowdown in D.C. was starting to affect NVR’s business. Obviously, D.C. makes up only a portion of the Mid-Atlantic numbers, but at 45% of revenue, the market is an important market for NVR, and this number could be an indicator of a potential slowdown in D.C.

NVR released Q2 2025 earnings on July 23, 2025. For the first six months of 2025, which ended on June 30, 2025, the Mid-Atlantic market had 4,579 new orders compared to 3,796 new orders this year. For the same period, NVR had 4,151 settlements this year compared to 4,165 settlements last year. So, new orders and settlements are both down slightly this year. Further, the average price for new orders is down from $525,900 in 2024 to $523,000 this year.

However, the average price for settlements this year is up to $532,600 so far in 2025 compared to $516,500 in 2024. A decline in new orders and settlements in the Mid-Atlantic market is something worth noting and worth keeping an eye on.

Despite all this, I am reluctant to call this a slowdown, with settlements coming in at 14 units less than last year, and with the average price of settlements still rising. While I am not naive to the fact that orders have slowed in the Mid-Atlantic market, I am skeptical to say these numbers are any indication of a slowdown in the D.C. housing market.

However, that does beg the question of how the housing market is holding up in D.C.

A D.C. Housing Market Story

According to Redfin, as of July 2025, the median sales price of a home in Washington, D.C. was $682,500, which is up 0.4% year-over-year. There were 629 homes sold, which was up 8.3% year-over-year. However, the median home was listed on the market for 57 days, which is up by 9 days year-over-year.

As of August 10, 2025, Bright MLS shows new listings are up 5.7% compared to last year, with the median sales price also up 0.8% compared to last year. They also show new contracts and showings are up 4.3% and 8% year-over-year, respectively.

In my view, these numbers seem to indicate a middling housing market. While none of these numbers seem to indicate to me a particularly strong housing market, I also do not see anything that leads me to believe there is a meaningful slowdown in demand for homes in D.C.

Housing data can tend to lag a bit when looking at economic indicators because it takes time to list a house, show a house, sell a house, pack up a house, find a house, buy a house, move out of a house, et cetra. So, if there really are a large number of unemployed workers looking to sell their houses at this moment, that may take several months to begin to show up in the housing market data.

However, I do not currently see a great deal of evidence indicating that might be the case. Nevertheless, when you consider the bizarre year D.C. is experiencing, the federal reserve considering cutting interest rates, and a national housing market that seems to be a mix of confusing, geographically divided, and a bit puzzling, the D.C. housing market is worth keeping an eye on in relation to NVR.

One Final Thought…

There is one final component to the question of whether or not NVR is being affected by all the bizarre events of 2025 in Washington, D.C. that keeps rattling around in my mind.

We know that D.C. accounts for a fair portion of NVR’s revenue, and that the D.C. market is a competitive and important market for NVR. So, what does NVR’s business in Washington, D.C. look like when we compare it to competitors?

Builder Magazine releases a report that compares home builders based on the number of closings they perform in a given year, as well as gross revenue. They use this data to attempt to get an estimated market share for home builders in a prospective area.

In 2014, Builder Magazine estimated NVR had a market share of 24.9% in the Washington D.C. region. And by 2024, they estimate that NVR has not only not lost market share, but they have grown to 26.1% market share in the region.

Market share is a particularly challenging aspect of business to sustain over long periods. New entrants enter the market, economic conditions fluctuate, customer preferences shift, and sometimes the markets in which a company sells can experience unexpected challenges. Like the multitude of challenges we have just reviewed. Nevertheless, I believe this is a crucial factor contributing to NVR’s strong position for investors with long-term horizons. If NVR can navigate through this kind of year in Washington, D.C., I am confident they can maintain profitability for years to come.

data.title: Zhihu Has Multiple Catalysts
data.description:
I'm still bullish about Zhihu Inc. (NYSE:ZH) (2390.HK) shares. Aggressive buybacks and meaningful financial improvement could allow the stock to command a more expensive valuation.

ZH's narrower-than-anticipated FY2024 losses were the subject of my prior March 28, 2025, update.

Q2 Marked A Big Turnaround

On Wednesday, August 27, the group issued a 6-K filing disclosing its financials for the latest three-month period.

ZH recorded a CNY 91M normalized net profit in April-June this year which surpassed expectations.

Analysts had predicted a 2Q25 net loss of -CNY 50M according to S&P Capital IQ. Its actual 2Q2024 bottom line was also a negative CNY 0.04B.

ZH's media release highlighted "monetization enhancements" and "improvements to our cost structure" that were reflected in its metrics.

"Gross Profit Margin" or "GPM" for the firm widened by +290bps (basis points) year-on-year and +70bps sequentially to 63% in the 2nd quarter of '25.

At its results briefing, ZH shared that "more than 40 premium short-form stories published on Zhihu (Chinese Quora-like Q&A site) had generated revenue (were monetized) through film and television licensing." Its intellectual property-related exploitation income more than doubled in the recent quarter on YoY terms.

I can see why its GPM got better, given the increase in contribution from high-margin IP-linked royalties.

Separately, ZH's OPEX-to-sales ratio declined from 79% in 2Q24 to 75% for 2Q2025.

The company credited the lower costs to "more disciplined promotional spending" and "improvements in our research and development efficiency" in its results announcement.

I think it can continue to stay in the black.

It has been loss-making in every single year during the 2021-2024 time frame following its March 2021 IPO. Looking ahead, ZH guided the analyst call that the Q2 outperformance "significantly increased the likelihood, the possibilities of reaching near breakeven on a full year non-GAAP basis."

My take is that the enterprise has margin enhancement drivers that are within its control.

In the past 3 months, it has done away with unnecessary expenses and pursued profitable revenue streams like IP adaptation. I'm confident that ZH can meet its FY25 financial goal by sticking to the same winning formula.

A Return To Positive Growth And Sustained Capital Return Are Potential Catalysts

The stock boasts a number of re-rating triggers in my opinion.

One of them is the likely realization of positive earnings for the current fiscal year which I highlighted in the preceding section. The other two are related to the firm's turnover and cash allocation.

ZH's earnings beat was largely linked to cost savings and higher margins rather than its topline performance.

The group saw its sales decrease 23% YoY to CNY 0.72B for 2Q25. As per S&P Capital IQ data, this was also 5% short of the sell-side's projection.

ZH explained in its press release that its revenues were hurt by the "proactive and ongoing refinement of service offerings to strategically focus on margin improvement."

The company should be getting closer to completing the internal business restructuring process as evidenced by its profitability improvement. It's highly probable that the top-line has already bottomed out.

Also, Zhihu has found new ways to unlock the value of its platform content, which should translate into additional revenues. As mentioned above, many movies and TV dramas have been or will be shot based on fiction posted by users on ZH's website.

My late-Mar '25 article touched on the possibility of the enterprise deriving "advertising revenue" and "usage fees" for its new "AI-enabled search tool called Zhihu Zhida."

The results call remarks suggest that ZH "will seek to integrate it (Zhihu Zhida) across our broader suite of products" once "it achieves critical penetration" rates and hits "key milestones." This sends a signal that "Zhihu Zhida" could be monetized in due course.

As such, I'm optimistic Zhihu's revenues will rise again by the latest next year.

Separately, ZH has turned more investor-friendly with the intention to return capital to stockholders.

It has a fresh one-year buyback plan that began on June 26, 2025, and expires on June 25 next year.

ZH can buy as many as one-tenth of its own shares under the current authorization. It has already used up 28% of this quota by the end of 1H25.

Zhihu revealed at the Q2 earnings meeting that it "continued to execute actively on the repurchase program" in July and noted that it "will stay the same course" for 2H2025. It also stressed that "we expect to remain one of the most significant U.S.-listed Chinese companies in terms of share repurchase ratio."

Judging by management comments, I won't bet against the firm executing on most of the authorized repurchases. This implies a potentially high-single digit percentage forward shareholder yield for ZH.

Variant Views You Should Consider

Investors would be disappointed in the scenario that Zhihu takes a longer-than-expected time to register positive profits or top-line increases.

Assuming that ZH finds other capital utilization options to be more attractive, it might make the decision to pause buybacks.

My Conclusion

Zhihu continues to be a deserving Buy-rated name.

Its forward price-to-sales is an undemanding 0.95 times based on S&P Capital IQ data.

ZH has a capital appreciation potential of +63% (1.55/0.95) by benchmarking it with peer Weibo Corporation (WB). The latter, which runs a social media platform by the same corporate name in China, trades at 1.55x P/S now.

Turnover growth, a reversal from losses, and consistent repurchases should help to catalyze the stock.

data.title: Microsoft: Buy Wonderful Businesses At Fair Prices
data.description:
MSFT stock: previous thesis and new developments

I last analyzed Microsoft Corporation's (MSFT) stock on July 9 in an article entitled “Microsoft: Record High Price Is A New Beginning (Technical Analysis).” The article rated the stock as a Buy with a focus on its technical trading indicators at the time. Since then, the company has released a new earnings report (ER) for its FQ4 2025 on July 30. As such, the remainder of this follow-up article will reexamine the stock based on these updates with a focus on its fundamentals. My overall assessment, echoing an old adage, is that MSFT’s current conditions represent an opportunity to buy a wonderful business at a fair price.

Let me start with a recap of its updated financials in the past quarter. As you can see from the following snapshot, Microsoft reported another robust quarter. The quarter featured broad strength across all earnings metrics. The growth from its two key segments, Azure and Office, is especially notable. For example, Azure saw growth of 39% with particular strength in cloud migration deals in the large enterprise segment. For its Office segment, Copilot's strength is moving the needle of growth judging by the financials, which is a driver I expect to continue in the years to come as detailed next.

MSFT stock: last dividend declaration

From the ER, some investors and analysts might be concerned with a higher CAPEX outlook (which I will revisit later), which could pressure its profitability metrics and growth investment. However, I am not too concerned as I see the higher CAPEX is backed by solid demand signals. As another sign to ease my concern on this front, Microsoft also declared its latest quarterly dividend in tandem with the ER, and the declaration keeps showing a double-digit annual growth rate. To wit, as seen from the table, the last declaration was $0.83 per share, with an ex-date of Aug 21 and represented a significant YOY increase of 11% from the $0.75 per share paid.

Finally, the company currently boasts such strong profitability metrics that some cost pressure can easily be absorbed in my model. As an example, the chart below shows my analysis of MSFT’s average cost of capital using the so-called WACC model detailed below, its ROE (return on common equity), and also ROCE (return on capital employed). As seen, its ROE (on average 31.5% in recent years) far exceeds its WACC (on average 9.3% in recent years). Moreover, in the case of MSFT, its ROCE in turn exceeds its ROE by a wide margin. For readers unfamiliar with ROCE, our blog article provides a detailed tutorial with Q&A. ROE can be limited or misleading in many cases as equity could include items that are NOT actually employed in a business's daily operation (such as cash reserves).

WACC represents a company's average after-tax cost of capital from all sources, including common stock, preferred stock, bonds, and other forms of debt. According to this model, MSFT’s WACC has been averaging around 9.43% in the past 10 years. Since the WACC is the minimal hurdle a company has to overcome to justify the use of capital, it is widely used as an approximation for the company’s discount rate.

MSFT stock: fair valuation estimate

MSFT’s latest ROCE is also highly competitive by horizontal comparison as seen in the next chart below, even among overachievers like the FAAMNG group. We have taken so many efforts to monitor its ROCE closely because our experience has taught us that the long-term growth of business fundamentally depends on two things: ROCE and the reinvestment rate (“RR”). This dependence has been detailed in our earlier articles so I will just quote the final results below:

The average ROCE for MSFT is around 67.3% in recent years. Our projection for MSFT’s reinvestment rate is about 17.5% for the years ahead (say the next ~5 years or so) given its current CAPEX outlook. With these inputs, MSFT’s growth rate would be ~11.8% (67.3% ROCE x 17.5% reinvestment rate = 11.8%).

At this point, two observations are noteworthy. First, note that this estimate is quite close to the latest dividend growth rate of 10.6% mentioned above. Dividend growth rates always serve as a reality check in our experience. Second, note that this growth estimation is also above the WACC analyzed above. If you (especially if you are a hard-core growth investor) believe its profit growth can sustainably exceed its WACC, a simple discounted cash flow model would show you that any valuation is justifiable in this case.

For those who don’t believe in this scenario, the table below shows the result from a two-stage discounted FCF (free cash flow) model. In this model, I assumed MSFT can sustain a growth rate of 11.8% CAGR for the next 10 years based on the ROCE and RR quoted above. Then in the 2nd stage, I assumed MSFT’s FCF to grow at a terminal rate of 6.4% afterward from a combination of 67.3% ROCE and 9.5% RR obtained from the averages of large mature tech companies. With these assumptions, the fair value of MSFT would be around $508, which is quite close to the current stock price.

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of downside risks, to revisit a point mentioned earlier, the rapid increase in CAPEX is a concern. Its accelerating CAPEX in recent quarters is a key factor for investors to consider. For example, as seen in its cash flow statement below, for the Sept quarter of 2023 (i.e., only ~2 years ago), the company's CAPEX stood at $9,917 million, which already represented a sizable outlay. The CAPEX requirements have intensified considerably to $17,079 million per its latest June ER, almost doubling the amount from about 2 years ago.

Of course, Microsoft's Cash From Operations (CFO) has also enjoyed robust growth in the meantime, as seen, rising from about $30 billion to the current $42 billion during this period. However, the increase in CAPEX far outpaced the growth of its CFO, as reflected in its CFO/CAPEX ratio shown in the second chart below. This ratio has experienced some fluctuation over the years, but the overall trend has been downward since 2023. For readers new to this ratio, a higher ratio is better as it means a small percentage of CFO is sufficient to meet the CAPEX needs. To wit, the average ratio is 3.063 in the past 5 years. In other words, the company requires about 1/3 of its cash flow from operations to support its capital expenditures on average in the past 5 years. Before 2023, the ratio was, on average, approximately 4x (i.e., it only required 1/4 of its CFO to support its CAPEX). As of the most recent ER, the ratio is 2.497, which means it spent about 40% of its CFO to support its CAPEX in the past quarter.

To close, this revisit of MSFT focused on the updates provided in its FQ4 ER. From experience, we learned that successful investing depends on a clear delineation of both long-term and near-term issues, a philosophy underlying the investment recommendations in our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement). In the case of MSFT, we consider the above issues to be temporary only. We further believe its current investment would only help to widen its moat in the long term, and the current valuation is fair when adjusted for its growth and profitability. These considerations have led me to reiterate my Buy rating.

data.title: Sabine Royalty Trust: High Yield, But The Clock Is Ticking
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Sabine Royalty Trust (NYSE:SBR) is an express trust made to receive the royalty and mineral interest payments from a fixed portfolio of oil and gas properties that were conveyed to it following its formation in 1982. SBR is currently paying quite an attractive yield, looking like an ideal way to play the oil and gas commodity cycle while receiving dividends for waiting on a rebound.

The company has a rather different business model, being more of a financial vehicle to transfer the royalties and other payments received as dividends to their shareholders, without a management team or employees. As we can see, the income took a hit during the last half a year as a result of lower oil prices, but they still made almost $36 million in distributable income during H1. Annualized, that’s ~$72 million, meaning a price to distributable income of around 14.5 during a bad environment for oil.

Most of their reserves are in the Permian - which is great given the low production cost these assets generally come with - but it’s also important to note that they have a limited life span. The company doesn’t actively manage and improve its portfolio, having a fixed asset base since it was formed in 1983. Its sole function is to collect the royalty income from the production of oil and gas on the current properties and pass that income directly to unitholders, after paying administrative expenses to Argent Trust Company, their trustee and basically the manager. They are explicitly and legally prohibited from acquiring new properties or making new investments, so don’t expect them to grow.

In fact, as their reserves are finally depleted, the trust is dissolved and its remaining assets are liquidated, which is a pre-determined and inevitable outcome of a depleting-asset investment. As the company says, the trust will be terminated either when there are two successive years of less than $2 million in revenue or following the vote of unitholders, after which the properties will be sold for market value and the cash received is distributed to unitholders.

The company’s trustee even stated back in June 2023 that:

With these estimated quantities of this year's reserve estimate of 5.9 million barrels of oil and 47.4 billion cubic feet of gas remaining, it could be estimated that the Trust still has a life span of 8 to 10 years.

So, SBR is likely to keep delivering solid dividends for the foreseeable future, especially if we see a slowdown in production that improves their life span by decreasing the depletion, or more discoveries as we’ve seen for the past more than 40 years, as I mention below.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see zero debt, as the company only has a few expenses and payables. There’s not much else to say about their financial position, as the trust corpus doesn’t reflect the current value of their assets and the most relevant factor about this company is the income combined with reserves, which I mentioned above.

Dividends & Buybacks

As the company pays basically everything they make after expenses as a monthly dividend, this can vary a lot. The company currently pays a significant yield of about 7.63% if we were to annualize the levels paid from January to August 2025, which is not terrible. However, it’s also important to note that these distributions are considered royalty income and are ordinary income, taxed at your marginal rate instead of the classic 15%, so you should be very careful if this yield is indeed something attractive for you.

Risks & Opportunities

With zero debt and monthly dividends, this is the type of investment that allows investors to enjoy the upside of oil while being rewarded with decent dividends for waiting - assuming that it’s bought at a good price.

For normal commodity producers, the cyclicality of their commodity has very significant effects, in some cases resulting in barely any growth throughout the entire cycle due to the damage a downside can cause. However, in SBR’s case as a royalty company, a downcycle usually results in lower profits, as the company still returns a positive number and doesn’t have to worry about significant CAPEX or cost savings, especially since they don’t have debt to worry about, unlike most big oil and gas producers. Again, since they’re not allowed to develop, this company won’t be able to do much other than pay dividends.

Another important point to remember about oil wells in particular is the combination of paraffin wax, asphaltenes, and mineral scales that make it hard and unsafe to close a well, which can have significant effects on the supply side of the cycle. These substances accumulate and create blockages that restrict the flow and prevent valves from closing properly, leading to significant operational challenges and potential to damage the well itself if closed. Instead, oil producers have the option of storing it and waiting for better prices instead - with oil storage being an industry that exploded when oil futures fell into the negative back in 2020, as a result of record-levels of demand for storing services in a very short time since nobody wanted to sell. So, in a way, we can see “pent-up supply” during the downcycle that prevents the upcycle from being great unless there is significant demand.

On the demand side, I believe rate cuts will be very important, combined with a potential conclusion on Trump’s tariffs (recently threatened India and China with tariff hikes again), plus OPEC+ eventually stopping the production hikes as well as China’s policy change boosting its economy - all being very positive factors for oil prices coming at basically the same time. Rate cuts (both in the US and China, alongside the rest of the world) in particular would be great since they would boost the economy, and since the US and China are by far the largest producers and consumers of oil, any cuts in these two countries could be excellent for oil demand. Meanwhile, since we’re getting into a CAPEX-cutting territory for many oil producers, the supply would be affected, potentially leading to higher oil prices in the future. Rate cuts would also make dividend stocks such as SBR more attractive, as they would result in lower treasury yields and force money to risk up if it wishes to get better yields.

This comes on top of the new equilibrium cost in 2030 being expected to reach even more than $55 per barrel based on some estimates, as new production will be more expensive going forward. As mentioned before, a recent Dallas Fed Energy Survey showed that to profitably drill a new well in the Permian Basin, companies needed prices of ~$64 per barrel, which is very close to the level we are at today:

For the entire sample, firms need $64 per barrel on average to profitably drill, higher than the $62-per-barrel price when this question was asked last year. Across regions, average breakeven prices to profitably drill range from $59 to $70 per barrel. Breakeven prices in the Permian Basin average $65 per barrel, $4 higher than last year. Almost all firms in the survey can profitably drill a new well at current prices. (The WTI spot price was $83 per barrel during the survey period.)

As I mentioned in the Dorchester Minerals (DMLP) stock analysis posted recently, the industry comes with a relatively unique aspect: the lag between production and cash receipts. Why that is an issue is because this delay can spread over multiple months as a result of adjustments, suspense releases, etc. and the prices can change dramatically during this time. In the example provided by DMLP above, they show how a typical cash receipt cycle looks like, assuming production in January, where we can see how the cash begins to flow over the next couple of months and can extend all the way to August, meaning a 7-month cycle from the start of cash receipts to receiving the full payments, with most of it coming in the first 2 months. What this means is we can see a small delay in good or bad times compared to oil producers - enough to have an effect on the next quarter, depending on the timing. The main thing to remember here is that just because they report a good quarter when oil companies have issues, it doesn’t mean they won’t follow - it’s just a delayed reaction.

Also, oil and gas has been going through a very intense period of mergers and acquisitions seen at all levels in recent years. This consolidation adds nothing to the total supply, and at best it generates synergies and better margins for the oil companies, but the effect on the supply of oil remains very limited. I say this because virtually every oil giant has been undergoing at least one significant merger/acquisition in the past years, including: Exxon's (XOM) Pioneer deal, Chevron (CVX) buying Hess, Occidental (OXY) + CrownRock, ConocoPhillips (COP) + Marathon Oil, etc.

However, the big issue here is that SBR is not actively managed and is not allowed to develop. Argent Trust Company acts as a trustee and is responsible for all the filing, payments of expenses/liabilities, etc. and has the fiduciary duty to act in the best interests of the unitholders. As the company’s end approaches, we have to be very careful how much we’re willing to pay.

SBR managed to catch an issue in the unpaid royalties of TGNR East Texas II, LLC recently, resulting in a $400,000 settlement that was paid as a dividend in March. However, since the company has to rely on the data given by their operators, it’s very hard to confidently say that there’s no other mistake.

Still, although the company is legally prohibited from expanding by buying other properties, that doesn’t mean they can’t expand their life span in any way. The company could always find more oil on their already owned properties, which can then increase the life of their assets - although that’s more of a gamble since we can’t really assume any value to this possibility. However, the trust has been expected to have a 9 to 10 years life span since inception back in 1982, and we’re still here talking about a similar life span now, more than 40 years later.

Valuation

As for the valuation, we’re talking about an asset that is inevitably running out. Two years ago, their trustee expected the reserves to run out in 8 to 10 years, which would be 6 to 8 now, but we should also take into account the possibility of discovering new reserves on their properties which can add several years to the equation. However, don’t count on endless discoveries. These assets are running out eventually.

The company even calculates a sort of DCF with a 10% discount rate that led them to $280.3 million at the end of last year, when commodity prices were significantly above the current ones, but that seems to be based on current proven reserves and production rates, which are unpredictable.

But again, this is ultimately about the yield each of us would be happy with. They’ve been paying an average of 8.44% since 2015, which seems like a fair expectation for an average if the assets aren’t depleted over the next decade. However, don’t forget to account for the taxes since this is considered royalty income.

Would you be happy with buying this stock close to its all-time highs despite low oil prices when the lifespan remains a gamble for this type of yield? Personally, I wouldn’t since there are better options out there such as DMLP, which I rated a Buy thanks to it being actively managed, high quality and the higher potential it comes with for similar dividend yields.

Conclusions

Overall, I can see the potential in SBR for income-focused portfolios given the attractive yield they come with, but given the limited potential and ticking life span of their assets, plus the fact that they’re not allowed to expand besides the discoveries made on their properties, I rate SBR a Hold now, as there are better plays in the oil and gas industry right now. I’ll still add it to my watchlist and look into buying on potential dips, however.

data.title: EFT: Lower Rates Will Cause Distributions To Be Reduced
data.description:
Overview

Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust (NYSE:EFT) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns from a diverse portfolio of floating rate loans. As the name implies, EFT puts emphasis on the floating rate structure within its portfolio. This helps the fund capitalize on the higher interest rate environment. An income fund like EFT can be an efficient way to offset the market volatility and uncertainty that traditional equities expose investors to. The fund has an inception dating back to 2004 and has a management fee of 1.17%.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that EFT's share price has declined by about 7.2%. Unfortunately, EFT has not been able to recover from the market decline that occurred earlier in the year. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return only sits around 1.8% over the same time frame. One of the main appeals of EFT is the high starting dividend yield of 8.8% and monthly payouts. However, I remain cautious about the sustainability of the payouts going forward.

Since the fund is exposed to assets that operate on a floating rate structure, the fund's outlook depends on whether or not interest rates are cut before the end of 2025. Lower interest rates may decrease the underlying earnings of the fund, but may also serve as a growth catalyst for EFT's share price. Therefore, timing of the entry can be a bit tricky and investors must assess their overall risk tolerance. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that EFT implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, EFT has total managed assets of $525.3M that are spread across 367 different loan issuers. The fund aims to provide broad exposure to the floating rate loan market by allocating assets across a wide range of sectors and credit tiers. Unlike common equities, EFT's underlying strategy doesn't rely on the earnings growth of its securities. Instead, EFT's investments simply need to continue maintaining debt payments, which is a more predictable investment to make. Therefore, the fund can be an efficient way to offset the uncertainty of equities.

Looking at the breakdown by industry, we can see that EFT is most heavily allocated to Software investments at 12.5% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Machinery and Health Care Providers, which accounts for 6.2% and 6% of assets respectively. This focus on diversity helps reduce any sort of concentration risk to any one focus area.

Looking at the top issuers, we can see that EFT provides investors with exposure to many private companies. For instance, UKG, Asurion, Boxer Parent Company, and Virgin Media Bristol are all private businesses at the moment. However, we must remember that EFT has exposure to these companies through debt, rather than common shares. Therefore, investors should limit their expectations of the fund's ability to participate in the growth of these underlying issuers.

Just to demonstrate this concept, I pulled in a performance chart of EFT over the last ten years. As we can see below, EFT's share price has declined by about 10.3% over the last decade, despite traditional market indices increasing. Therefore, investors should anticipate that their returns are likely to come exclusively from the distributions that are received. For instance, the total return when including distributions jumps up to 92.6% over the last ten years.

Valuation

Since EFT is structured as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, EFT now trades at a slight discount to NAV of approximately 4.7%. For reference, EFT has traded at an average discount to NAV of 4.88% over the last three year period. While a discount can potentially indicate an attractive opportunity to accumulate shares, it really depends on the fund's history. To better conceptualize the current discount level in relation to its ten-year range, I added the red line in the chart below. EFT seems to more frequently trade at a much larger discount, so entry here may not be ideal.

To get a better understanding of the internal performance of the fund, I reviewed the latest annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $1.21 per share, while net realized losses were $0.47 per share. Looking back on prior years, we can see that net investment income levels are much higher than they were prior to interest rate hikes. For instance, EFT's net investment income was only $0.72 per share in 2020, which is when interest rates were cut to near zero levels. A higher interest rate means that EFT can collect a higher level of interest income from its underlying loans.

However, net investment income isn't always sufficient enough to cover the distributions that the fund pays. This causes EFT to be reliant on net realized gains to make up the difference. The only issue is that net realized gains are dependent on positive market conditions so that EFT can sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. Therefore, it can be difficult for EFT to capture positive gains during periods where market indexes are declining. This reliance on net realized gains has made it difficult for the fund to experience positive NAV growth over time. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $14.56 per share
  • 2022: $13.24 per share
  • 2023: $12.70 per share
  • 2024: $13.32 per share
  • 2025: $12.81 per share

However, I think the NAV has still held up pretty well over time. The fund's consistent net investment income levels have demonstrated that EFT can efficiently generate income from its holdings. I believe that future interest rate cuts can actually serve as a growth catalyst over the long term. However, a lower interest rate may also cause net investment income to decline over the short-term, since EFT will be able to collect a lower amount of interest income from its holdings.

Although earnings may see a slight decrease, management can offset this by investing into new opportunities. A lower interest rate environment may encourage an influx of new borrowers to seek debt financing, which presents growth potential for EFT. Additionally, the lower cost of debt would improve the fund's operating spread, as well as the operating margins for holdings. As we can see below, EFT's share price rapidly increased throughout 2020 and 2021 when interest rates were cut to near zero levels following the pandemic.

Risk Profile

As part of the fund's operating strategy, EFT utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings. The amount of leverage currently amounts to 21.5% of assets, which I consider to be a moderate amount of leverage during this environment of elevated interest rates. However, investors must consider the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated to investments that don't perform as anticipated. Since interest rates are high, the spread between EFT's cost of leverage and the yield generated from securities is also thinner.

Another source of risk would be the exposure to securities with poor credit ratings. For reference, securities with a credit rating below a BBB are classified as below investment grade. These are typically borrowers with less consistent cash flows or that are more reliant on debt to fuel operations. The large exposure to below investment grade borrowers means that EFT can be exposed to default risk. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 2.94%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 93.14%
  • Not Rated: 3.92%

In a scenario where interest rates remain elevated, EFT can be impacted by rising defaults. If borrowers struggle to keep up with their required debt maintenance, EFT's share price can decline. For instance, S&P Global estimates that the leveraged loan default rate can rise as high as 3.5% by March of 2026. This assumes a scenario where there is slowing economic growth and continued trade disruptions related to tariffs.

Dividend Is Likely To Decline

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.092 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.8%. This latest declaration represented a small 2.1% decline in payouts. If we multiply this monthly distribution by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.104 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, EFT has demonstrated that it is capable of supporting these distributions when market conditions are favorable. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2021: $2.26 per share
  • 2022: ($0.45) per share
  • 2023: $0.51 per share
  • 2024: $2.02 per share
  • 2025: $0.74 per share

As we can see, EFT only generated a sufficient level of earnings to support the current payout in two of the last five years. The market seems to be anticipating interest rate cuts to occur as soon as September, related to core CPI data. Since lower interest rates are likely to lead to lower net investment income, I anticipate EFT's distributions to be reduced over the rest of 2025. As we can see from the dividend history below, EFT has already started cutting its distributions over the course of 2025.

We don't know how large interest rate cuts will be, so there's a chance that cuts don't get much larger. These variable payouts are just a natural result of floating rate loans and shouldn't be interpreted as an underlying issue of the fund. Since interest rates are anticipated to trend lower, it becomes difficult to recommend EFT for income focused investors right now.

Lastly, the annual report indicates that distributions tend to be fueled by net investment income. Therefore, the dividends received from EFT are classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax burden for investors. So based on your specific tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, and desired income level, a position in EFT may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset the potential tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, now might not be the best time to initiate a position in EFT. However, I don't think that existing shareholders necessarily have a reason to sell out of their position right now. Lower interest rates may lead to lower distributions, but it can also lead to a rising share price over time. The fund has also maintained attractive returns over the last decade and proved that it can navigate a changing interest rate environment in the past. The fund maintains diversity across a wide range of industries, which helps limit concentration risk. Additionally, lower interest rates can create a more favorable borrowing environment for businesses, which EFT can capitalize on by investing into new opportunities.

data.title: Micron: Strong Buy On AI-Driven HBM Growth
data.description:
Micron Technology's (NASDAQ:MU) stock has rallied 25.3% since my last report, sharply outperforming the S&P 500’s 12.2% gain. The stock has yet to reach my previously established price target of roughly $135, but in this report, I will analyze the expectations for Q4 2025 earnings and adjust my rating to "Strong Buy" based on a bullish guidance update.

What Wall Street Expects For Micron Technologies In Q4 2025 Earnings

Analysts are expecting the company to report $11.05 billion in sales, up 42.6% year-on-year and 19% higher sequentially. The revision trend for Micron Technology has been positive, with revenue adjustments being revised upward by roughly 12% over the past three to six months. From the 25 Wall Street analysts providing estimates, there have been 24 upward revisions and one revision downward.

Earnings per share are expected to climb 141.7% year-over-year to $2.85 with a 36%-41% improvement over the past three to six months. In the prior quarter, the company realized earnings per share of $1.91, with all 18 analysts providing EPS estimates having revised their EPS expectations upward.

We've seen a slight improvement in the estimates over the past three months, with 19 analysts revising their estimates up and 15 revising their estimates downward. In the prior quarter, the company booked EPS of $0.81.

Encouraging for shareholders and Micron Technology is the fact that the company has beat estimates on earnings and sales every quarter for the past two years.

Micron Technology Updates Guidance Before Quarter End

On Aug. 11, Micron Technology updated its guidance upward, lifting the revenue estimate for Q4 from $10.4-$11 billion to $11.1-$11.3 billion, driven by strong improvements in pricing particularly driven by DRAM sales. Volumes were stable compared to the planned shipping plan, and while there may be some pull forward to stay ahead of tariffs, the volumes shipped being in line with expectations largely shows that the top line growth was primarily driven by improved pricing. As a result, gross margins are expected to be 43%-44% compared to earlier guidance of 40%-42%. This led to an EPS revision from $2.35-$2.65 to $2.78-$2.92.

Compared to the estimates from analysts, we see that analysts actually have their sales estimates at the lower end of the guided range, and their EPS estimates match the midpoint of the updated range that Micron provided.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Micron Technology?

Like for any company, Micron Technology has to manage its inventories very well. Particularly on NAND, there has been some weakness driven by competition from China and softer demand for chips from the automotive industry. Micron has adjusted its capacity permanently to reduce the risk of being stuck with high inventories.

For DRAM, demand remains robust, driven by data center growth supporting AI expansion. For DRAM, node transitions offer an opportunity. The 1-Beta node launched in 2022 still uses deep ultraviolet multiple pattern technologies, but offers significantly higher bit density compared to 1-Alpha. The company is now working on the production ramp-up for the 1-gamma node that uses EUV technology, which allows an even higher bit density.

The company’s HBM chips, a specialized form of DRAM, offer growth opportunities for AI accelerators, data centers, and high-performance computing demand.

Overall, the company has some risk in NAND, where softness has already been detected and the company adjusted to that. A risk for the DRAM business would be any stalling in demand in the data center and AI part of the business.

Micron Technology Stock Is A "Strong Buy"

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Micron Technology, I am upgrading my rating from "Buy" to "Strong Buy" with a price target of $146.88. That is not because the stock is cheap compared to its median EV/EBITDA. but because the company remains significantly undervalued compared to peers, while the company should be in a net cash position next year. That is a combination that is not easily found. For this year, the EBITDA estimates have increased 5.5%, 5.9% for FY26, and 7.1% for FY27. This lifts the CAGR from 41.4% to 45.1%. So, an already strong CAGR became even stronger, and total EBITDA generation is expected to be 6.3% higher than previously modeled.

Free cash flow is expected to grow strongly in the years ahead with high bandwidth memory as the key driver, and that allows the company to continue expanding its HBM capabilities and gain market share again. The net cash position expected next year could also allow the company to return value to shareholders. One thing I do like is that when asked about shareholder returns during the Q3 2025 earnings call, the CFO clearly outlined that investing in technology leadership was the priority.

Conclusion: Micron Technology's Healthy Balance Sheet And Technology Leadership Make It Attractive

Micron Technology remains undervalued against peers, and while we note that at times inventory surpluses have hurt the company’s business, we also see that the company is now proactively adjusting NAND capacity and focusing on expanding market share for DRAM and particularly HBM. It is doing so while managing the balance sheet carefully, allowing the business to grow through investing in product development, and possibly, although not prioritized, the company could use excess cash to repurchase shares. Even without share repurchases, I believe the stock is extremely compelling.

data.title: BILL Holdings: The Turnaround Is Here
data.description:
One clear theme has rung out as we conclude the majority of the Q2 earnings season: investors are largely ditching small and mid-cap stocks, preferring to rush to the perceived safety of large-cap growth. In my view, this has created risk at the top of the market. But just because the S&P 500 (SP500) is expensive, dominated by the likes of Microsoft (MSFT) and Nvidia (NVDA), doesn't mean that we have to wait for the valuation hammer to eventually drop: I'm continuing to emphasize rotating more of my portfolio into overlooked small and mid-cap names that offer growth at a reasonable price.

BILL Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:BILL) aka Bill.com, the parent company behind payment processing and payroll company Bill.com, is one of these companies. Bill.com just reported very strong fiscal Q4 results and issued robust guidance for the year ahead, helping to kick off a rebound rally in a stock that was a market darling during the height of the pandemic. Still, Bill.com remains down ~40% since the start of the year, driven by lingering concerns over the company's sales execution: so investors still have a chance to get in on the ground floor here.

I last wrote a Strong Buy opinion on Bill.com in June, when the company was still trading in the low $40s. Since then, we like the fact that Bill.com has delivered clarity on its growth trajectory on the upcoming FY26, for which the company is still pointing to double-digit growth in its core revenue, helping to offset the risk of weaker float revenue from lower interest rates. On top of this, Bill.com's TPV (total payment volumes) in its network continue to grow, which is impressive in a tightening macroeconomy with weaker consumer spending. I'm resetting my rating to a Buy here (given the recent upside in the stock), but am more than happy to hold onto this stock for further gains.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to this name, here is what I view to be the long-term bull case for Bill.com:

  • Steady buildup of "core revenue" reduces Bill.com's reliance on float balances. One of the main reasons Bill.com has slid over the past year is due to the fear of lower interest rates, which feeds a portion of the company's revenue. But at the same time, the company's "core revenue," consisting of subscription fees plus transactional fees, has grown to ~90% of the company's revenue, helping to insulate the company from interest rate shocks.
  • Double-digit TPV growth. Bill.com is growing its total payment volumes at a low-teens pace, capturing more omnichannel spending from its customers and helping to drive transactional revenue.
  • Broad customer base with little concentration. Bill.com has amassed a massive customer base of nearly 0.5 million customers, reducing its reliance on any single customer and continuing to add new business every quarter.
  • Sky-high gross margins enabling economies of scale. Bill.com enjoys pro forma gross margins in the ~85% range, which is best-in-class for a software company and enables it to generate significant economies of scale as it grows.

Despite BILL's strengths, the stock still remains quite reasonably priced. At current post-earnings share prices near $48, Bill.com trades at a market cap of $4.81 billion. Netting off the $2.22 billion of cash against $1.71 billion of debt on Bill.com's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $4.30 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY26 (the year for Bill.com ending in June 2026), the company is guiding to $1.59-$1.63 billion in revenue, or 9-11% y/y growth. Underlying core revenue, meanwhile, is expected to grow 12-15% y/y (reaching 91% of total revenue at the midpoint), partially offset by the expectation of lower interest rates and lower float revenue.

The company is also expecting $240-$270 million pro forma operating income, the midpoint of which signals a 15.8% pro forma operating margin (-60bps y/y, primarily driven by the fact that "free" contribution from float revenue is expected to decrease). If we assume a similar -60bps reduction to FCF margins to 20.8% (still above 20.0% in FY24), FCF on the midpoint of Bill.com's revenue guide would be $333.6 million (+7% y/y).

This positions Bill.com's valuation multiples at:

  • 2.7x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 12.9x EV/FY26 FCF
  • 22.9x FY26 P/E.

To me, the opportunity to buy a high-margin software company growing in the low-teens at a <3x revenue and <15x FCF multiple is quite rare in today's market, and offers a great bulwark against the expensiveness of the large-cap S&P 500. Stay long here and keep riding the rebound rally higher.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Bill.com's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Bill.com's revenue grew 12% y/y to $383.3 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $376.2 million (+9% y/y) by a three-point margin. Revenue growth actually accelerated one point versus 11% y/y growth in Q3, which is notable as Bill.com faces two specific headwinds: a weaker macroeconomy that should be weighing down on transactions, as well as lower interest rates. Float revenue of $37.4 million declined -12% y/y, while core revenue jumped 15% y/y to $345.9 million.

Bill.com has been able to muscle past macroeconomic unease by landing a raft of new customers. As shown in the chart below, the company's total customer count hit 493.8k in the quarter, adding 5.2k net-new customers and growing the customer base at a 4% y/y clip. Meanwhile, total payment volumes of $86.1 billion jumped 13% y/y. The company notes that its network powers roughly about 1% of annual U.S. GDP.

New products, especially geared for the company's larger and more complex clients, have been a particular driver of growth. The company recently released a new procurement platform, combining accounts payable/receivable, spend & expense, procurement, and forecasting into a single platform. The company notes as well that AI features have been a major upsell point for its customer base, with more than 40k (just shy of 10%) of the company's customers using two or more AI features. Per CEO Rene Lacarte's remarks on the Q4 earnings call regarding Bill.com's AI releases:

Since the beginning of 2025, BILL's AI solution has increased the number of fully automated bills by 80%. We are also increasing access to capital which is critical to success of SMBs. In fiscal 2025, our AI features helped us to proactively identify when customers qualify for larger credit limits, enabling us to extend $200 million in proactive line increases for spend and expense customers. And BILL's AI-enabled fraud solutions protect the most important asset SMBs have, their cash. In FY '25, our predictive AI solutions helped us stop over 8 million fraudulent attempts. BILL's platform is proactive, predictive and acts as the intelligence layer that powers financial operations across SMBs. We are investing in Agentic AI, building a new generation of intelligent agents that would deliver autonomous finance for SMBs and accelerate the shift from doing it with you to doing it for you. To be clear, we're not just adding Agentic AI into workflows. We're eliminating the workflows themselves."

On the profitability side, Bill.com has boosted its full-year pro forma operating margin to 16.4% in FY25, up 120bps y/y from 15.2% in FY24:

Meanwhile, in FY25, Bill.com's free cash flow soared 21% y/y to $312.5 million, representing a 21.4% margin that improved 140bps y/y:

Risks and key takeaways

Despite proving for several quarters in a row that Bill.com is stabilizing its customer retention and top-line growth, the stock remains quite cheap at a low-teens multiple of FCF. In an acknowledgement as well that management believes the stock is undervalued, Bill.com is also commencing a new share repurchase program for up to $300 million in buybacks, representing ~6% of the company's current market cap.

Of course, there are risks to bear in mind here. Bill.com's large orientation toward SMB customers makes it particularly vulnerable amid a macro slowdown (especially when consumers are shopping less and eating out less frequently, many more mom and pop establishments may go under).

Still, I think Bill.com's risk is already well compensated for in its low valuation multiples. Buy here and stay long.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: 'Economy Is Slowing, But Still Clearly Growing'
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Lumber futures on the edge of bear territory (0:20) Snowflake, HP gaining post-earnings (1:25) U.S. GDP growth revised higher (2:30) Pattern Group Nasdaq IPO (3:45) Teva's GLP-1 weight loss drug (4:40)

The following is an abridged transcript:

Our top story so far

Does a bear … lurk in the woods?

Lumber futures (LB1:COM) are on the edge of bear territory. The initial tariff-driven upswing has fizzled out and cracks in the housing market are beginning to show.

Lumber has retreated from an early August high of about $695 per thousand board feet to about $560. That’s a decline of nearly 19.5%, just shy of the 20% drop that signals a technical bear market.

Lumber futures saw a surge driven by tariffs and optimism over lower interest rates, which pushed prices to their highest levels in more than three years. But enthusiasm soon faded, as recent housing data disappointed and builders scaled back due to higher input costs, weaker demand and looming affordability challenges.

BofA says housing affordability remains stretched even with potential rate cuts, requiring better wage growth or increased supply for meaningful improvement.

Mill closures, reduced North American production capacity and tariff concerns have resulted in increased volatility in the lumber market of late.

Among active stocks:

Snowflake (SNOW) is surging post-earnings, with accelerating sales and momentum from its artificial intelligence strategy prompting analysts to increase price targets and reiterate strong ratings.

Goldman Sachs analysts said: "We highlight that Snowflake has an opportunity to be a Gen-AI platform, enabling customers to develop agentic applications using Cortex AISQL."

HP (HPQ) is among the top S&P 500 gainers after its quarterly results and guidance topped expectations despite tariff concerns.

Evercore analysts said: "Although print market fundamentals remain somewhat soft, continued PC strength provides an offset which should enable HPQ’s top line to remain positive over the next few quarters,"

And Best Buy (BBY) topped Q2 estimates and set favorable guidance, but predicted a slight tightening of margins.

Best Buy sees fiscal 2026 revenue of $41.1 billion to $41.9 billion (midpoint $41.5 billion) vs. $41.4 billion consensus and EPS of $6.15 to $6.30 (midpoint $6.225) vs. $6.14 consensus.

On the economic front:

U.S. GDP growth was revised higher, to an annual rated of 3.3% from 3% at the initial estimate.

The revision primarily reflects upward revisions to investment and consumer spending, which were partly offset by a downward revision to government spending and an upward revision to imports.

Q2 PCE prices were revised to a 2% increase, down from the initial estimate of 2.1% and 3.7% in Q1. Core PCE prices, though, stayed at 2.5% vs. the prior estimate.

Heather Long, chief economist at Navy Federal, says: “Bottom line: Economy is slowing, but still clearly growing.”

And the July Pending Home Sales Index edged down 0.4% for the month to 71.7, missing the consensus of a 0.2% rise. Sales in July increased 0.7% from a year ago, compared with a 2.8% annual decline in June.

NAR Chief Economist Lawrence Yun says: "Even with modest improvements in mortgage rates, housing affordability, and inventory, buyers still remain hesitant."

And in other news of note:

E-commerce firm Pattern Group (PTRN) has filed for an IPO on the Nasdaq.

Pattern Group was originally founded as iServe in 2013. By 2018, the company rebranded from iServe to Pattern and expanded internationally with the acquisition of the consultancy Practicology.

The Utah-based company uses advanced AI and machine learning to manage logistics, pricing, advertising, and customer service for its partners. Its specialty is managing branded sales on digital marketplaces like Amazon (AMZN), Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT) and TikTok Shop.

Pattern Group (PTRN) disclosed that it generated revenue of $1.80 billion for the year that ended on Dec. 31, 2024, which represents a compound annual growth rate of 35% over the last two years. Notably, Pattern Group derived 94% of its revenue from consumer product sales on Amazon. In addition, Amazon accounted for over 75% of the total U.S. marketplace retail sales in 2024.

And Teva Pharmaceuticals (TEVA) said that the FDA approved its generic version of Novo Nordisk's (NVO) Saxenda, a GLP-1 weight loss drug.

“With this approval, and by launching a generic for Saxenda, we will provide patients in the U.S. the first ever generic GLP-1 product specifically indicated for weight loss,” the company said.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Copper And Silver Are Setting Up For Powerful Moves (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

I’ve been spotting and writing a lot about volatility squeezes lately, which occur when volatility drops to extremely low levels and signal big upcoming moves. This dynamic is currently developing in assets such as gold and U.S. Treasuries.

These volatility squeezes are not coincidences, as they are actually all related. Much of the financial markets are in a gigantic volatility squeeze as they wait for a major catalyst.

These potential catalysts include further clarity on the U.S. stagflation situation (inflation + economic stagnation), the likelihood of further rate cuts, and the next Fed chair that President Trump will appoint.

In today’s update, I want to focus on the volatility squeeze developing in copper and how it is setting up both copper and its closely correlated counterpart, silver, for a significant move.

Let’s first take a look at the weekly chart of London Metal Exchange (LME) copper futures, which I am now using as my preferred proxy for copper prices instead of U.S. COMEX copper futures because the latter have been heavily distorted by the Trump administration’s recent tariffs.

This chart shows a clear volatility squeeze as indicated by the Bollinger Band Width indicator beneath the chart, which is a valuable tool for tracking volatility in financial markets and assets.

The last time volatility in copper dropped to such extremely low levels was in 2022 and 2023, and in both instances, it led to significant moves in copper prices.

Based on the current setup, I believe another major move in copper is ahead as this volatility squeeze resolves.

While volatility squeeze setups indicate that a big move is likely ahead, they do not reveal the direction of that move. To determine the likely outcome, we need to incorporate other methods, including chart analysis.

The weekly chart of LME copper futures currently shows an ascending triangle pattern forming, which is typically a bullish chart pattern. However, it must be confirmed by a decisive breakout on strong volume above the $10,800 per tonne resistance level.

This combination of an ascending triangle and a volatility squeeze indicates that a significant bullish move in copper is likely ahead.

The longer-term monthly chart of LME copper futures highlights both the ascending triangle pattern that has been developing over the past several years and the critical $10,000 to $10,800 per tonne resistance zone, which dates all the way back to 2011.

Once copper achieves a decisive breakout from both its ascending triangle and above this major resistance zone, I believe a powerful new bull market in copper will begin.

For those interested in this bullish copper thesis, one convenient way to gain exposure is through the Global X Copper Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:COPX).

Interestingly, COPX has also been forming an ascending triangle pattern since 2021 and is now on the verge of breaking out. However, I would prefer to see a decisive close above the $48 resistance level for stronger confirmation of the breakout.

In addition to the bullish technical setup in copper, the fundamental picture is also highly supportive. For example, Goldman Sachs has dubbed copper "the new oil" due to its essential role in clean energy technologies, and Visual Capitalist published a fascinating infographic on this theme.

Copper earns this title because its demand is expected to surge in the coming decades, while oil consumption is projected to decline as the world transitions away from fossil fuels. Reflecting this shift, the IMF forecasts a 66% increase in copper demand between 2020 and 2040.

Copper’s likely upcoming bull market would align with the outlook of French billionaire and commodities trader Pierre Andurand, who predicted that copper prices could soar to $40,000 per tonne in the coming years—a more than fourfold increase from the current price of $9,682 per tonne.

Explaining his bullish stance, Andurand stated, “We are moving towards a doubling of demand growth for copper due to the electrification of the world, including electric vehicles, solar panels, wind farms, as well as military usage and data centers.”

If copper enters a major bull market, it would also be highly bullish for silver because silver, along with gold, is heavily influenced by the prices of both metals. This relationship exists largely due to arbitrage trading algorithms that tend to pull silver in the same direction as gold and copper.

Because of this little-known dynamic, I developed a proprietary indicator called the Synthetic Silver Price Index (SSPI).

The SSPI is essentially the average of gold and copper prices, and it has proven to track silver extremely closely, making it a powerful tool for both confirming and predicting moves in silver, as I’ll demonstrate shortly.

Interestingly, a volatility squeeze is also forming in the SSPI, which reflects the volatility squeezes occurring in its underlying components, gold and copper.

As shown in the chart below, previous volatility squeezes in the SSPI have consistently led to significant moves in the indicator—and, by extension, in silver as well. I view the current volatility squeeze in the SSPI as a strong signal that a major move in silver is approaching.

Zooming in on the daily chart of the Synthetic Silver Price Index (SSPI) shows that an ascending triangle pattern is forming (there are a lot of these lately!).

Once this pattern breaks out, it is likely to result in a significant bullish move, which would also place substantial upward pressure on silver.

The chart below illustrates just how closely correlated my proprietary Synthetic Silver Price Index (SSPI) and silver are. Since 2018, the correlation has been an impressive 95%, which is remarkable given that silver is not even a component of the SSPI.

One of my favorite ways to use the SSPI is to perform technical analysis on it and treat its breakouts and breakdowns as signals for what silver is likely to do next. This approach has proven to be highly effective and, in many cases, the SSPI even leads the price of silver.

For this reason, the current volatility squeeze and ascending triangle pattern forming in the SSPI are particularly noteworthy and well worth watching for silver bulls.

I mentioned earlier that the volatility squeeze in the Synthetic Silver Price Index (SSPI) is the byproduct of the volatility squeezes in its two components, copper and gold. I’ve already shown the setup in copper, so now let’s look at the triangle pattern that has been forming in gold over the past five months.

I am now waiting for a decisive breakout in gold. Assuming that occurs, I expect it to run to $4,000 and beyond, which would, in turn, boost both the SSPI and silver itself.

With all this discussion about how bullish moves in copper and gold would be highly supportive for silver, let’s finally take a closer look at silver itself.

I believe silver began a major new bull market in early June when it broke above the $32 to $35 resistance zone that had capped its progress for much of the previous year.

Since that breakout, silver has been consolidating and forming a triangle pattern of its own. Despite this sideways action, I believe the bull market thesis remains intact. Silver has simply been taking a healthy breather, which is normal during the summer months when much of Wall Street is on vacation.

Looking ahead, the combination of summer ending and the potential for major breakouts in both copper and gold is what I believe will propel silver’s bull market much higher—to $50, $60, and far beyond. However, confirmation is still needed, which would come in the form of silver closing above the key $40 resistance level.

To summarize, the extremely quiet trading that has characterized Summer 2025 has actually created multiple volatility squeezes across key assets, including U.S. Treasuries, gold, and copper.

Ironically, periods of extremely low volatility almost always resolve with extremely high volatility, and I believe we are likely to see that happen in these assets soon.

While many investors are bored with these assets, and most don’t even understand what volatility squeezes are, they should be doing the exact opposite.

This is shaping up to be a very exciting setup and opportunity for precious metals investors, as big rallies are likely to erupt this fall.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: MP Materials: The Defense Establishment Comes A-Calling
data.description:
After having clamped down on rear earth element exports earlier this year, China restarted those exports in July after a de-escalation in trade tensions. However, the Chinese government also warned Western companies not to stockpile large inventories as attempts to do so would be met with another prolonged cut-off. Over the last decade, the deterioration in US-China relations has finally led China to make a strategic move that had been years in the making.

In a famous speech in 1992, then-Chinese leader Deng Xiaoping stated: “The Middle East has its oil, China has rare earth: China’s rare earth deposits account for 80 percent of identified global reserves, you can compare the status of these reserves to that of oil in the Middle East: it is of extremely important strategic significance; we must be sure to handle the rare earth issue properly and make the fullest use of our country’s advantage in rare earth resources.”

And during the next decade and a half, China followed Deng’s call to action by ramping rare earth element (“REE”) production to such an extent that by the mid-2000s it was practically the world's only supplier. Furthermore, Chinese producers pursued an integrated approach that included both production and refining.

MP Yesterday

Needless to say, this wasn’t good news for American producers like The Molybdenum Corporation of America, which owned and operated the Mountain Pass rare earth mine in California. Long story short, the mine shut down in 2002, was revived in 2007, put into care and maintenance in 2015, and revived again in 2017 by MP Materials (NYSE:MP).

But after a diplomatic spat with Japan in 2010 resulted in China halting its exports of rare earths to that country, most of the world’s major industrialized nations quickly realized their predicament. This set off a round of exploration and development efforts in numerous mining jurisdictions around the world to restart REE output.

However, even after MP brought Mountain Pass back online in 2017, it wasn’t all lollipops and sunshine as NdPr price swings and strong Chinese production caused MP’s stock price to ebb and flow. But all that now looks like ancient history as recent events have brought much greater attention to this oft-forgotten corner of the metals market. Efforts to foster domestic sourcing have increased as the new US administration has taken a more strident tone in matters of trade and resource independence. Naturally, this has led to a lot of eyes turning to MP Materials and its Mountain Pass property.

MP Today

The Mountain Pass mine is the main domestic source of several REEs and continues to provide decent yields even after 60 years of drilling. At a 2% cut-off, its bastnaesite concentrate provides a TREO (total rare earth oxide) of cerium, lanthanum, neodymium-praseodymium (“NdPr”), samarium, europium, and gadolinium in the following amounts:

As of December 31, 2024, the mine had a Proven Reserve of 810k Short Tons with a grade of 4.06% TREO and a Probable Reserve of 28.88M ST grading 6.02%. It had an Indicated Resource of 4.35M ST grading 3.71% TREO and an Inferred Resource of 13.35M ST with an average grade of 4.79% TREO. The Resource and Reserve are mutually exclusive. The company estimates Life of Mine at 29 years, but the ample Resource leads one to believe that there’s room for LOM extension.

In recent years, MP’s management also made a concerted effort to capture a greater share of the downstream value-add premium for its metal. It appears to be taking a page out of Materion’s (MTRN) playbook and the long-term strategy that company has utilized to dominate the beryllium value chain.

When the company reported Q2 results in early August, management announced that MP had stopped selling concentrate to third parties and would instead stockpile excess production until NdPr oxide refining operations are fully ramped. As can be seen in the exhibit below, the new product categories, coupled with higher REE prices, helped MP show greatly improved numbers over those of corresponding periods last year.

MP Tomorrow

But the company’s Q2 numbers don’t paint the full picture as the march of events promises to shape MP into a very different company within the next few years. In July, the miner signed a deal with Apple (AAPL) to supply the iPhone maker with magnets produced with recycled feedstock; the deal is valued at $500 million.

Now, normally that would be enough; usually, when the CEO of a relatively small mining company signs a sizable deal with the downstream producer of a brand-name product, they will wax lyrically about it forever and a day. For example, when Livent Corp, a lithium miner that was later purchased by Rio Tinto (RIO), signed a long-term $600 million lithium hydroxide supply deal with GM in 2022, the company crowed about it for years.

However, MP’s Apple deal is practically a footnote when compared to its agreement with the DOD. As most readers are probably well aware by now, last month, MP signed a supply deal that will see the Defense Department take an equity stake in the company through the issuance of $400 million of convertible preferred equity and warrants. A full exercise of the warrants and conversion feature would allow the Department of Defense to become the largest shareholder in MP Materials.

Also included is a $150 million low interest loan to fund the expansion of MP’s heavy-rare-earth separation circuit at Mountain Pass. MP will expand its magnet manufacturing capacity by 10X through the construction of a facility that has been dubbed the 10X Facility, and the DOD committed to buy all new production coming from that plant on a cost-plus basis, which includes a $140 million minimum EBITDA guarantee. The deal includes a $110/kg price floor for all products containing NdPr.

Takeaway

Unsurprisingly, MP’s share price has surged since the announcement of both these deals, going from the low-$20 per share range, where the stock traded for most of the first half of the year, to surging as high as the mid-70s in mid-August; MP currently trades at an enterprise value of $12.3 billion. The sharp jump in the share price is enough to give me pause and is the reason why I rate the stock as a Hold.

But what's going on at MP is extremely interesting and definitely merits keeping an eye on. Cooling US-China relations over the last decade are leading the US government to adopt an increasingly active industrial policy. The Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 kicked off the process through targeted funding for certain sectors, and the DOD’s purchase of MP Materials’ equity points to an acceleration in this trend. There also appears to be a consensus between both major political parties on this issue.

In my opinion, geopolitical tensions are likely to keep rising; after all, both China and the US want to be the global hegemon, but as the Highlander famously said: There can only be one. This means that I’ll be keeping a close eye on MP’s share price and revisiting the stock on any major pullback.
